findings.summary: Inline comments restate what the code already says, adding no insight
evidence: environment.py line 490: `# Set matplotlib backend for headless operation` immediately before `os.environ["MPLBACKEND"] = "Agg"` — the comment echoes the assignment
environment.py line 526: `# Check for .venv directory` before `venv_path = repo_root / ".venv"` — identical to what the next line does
environment.py line 531: `# Check for uv.lock file` before `lock_file = repo_root / "uv.lock"` — same pattern
logging_utils.py line 23: `# Import from split modules` is trivially obvious from the import statements that follow
findings.suggestion: Remove inline comments that duplicate the adjacent code. Keep only comments that explain WHY (e.g., why Agg is needed in headless mode) rather than WHAT the code does.

findings.summary: Multi-line docstrings with Args/Returns/Example blocks on trivial 2-5 line functions
evidence: logging_utils.py lines 329-343: `log_success` has a 9-line docstring (Args, Example) for a 3-line function body that just prefixes a string with an emoji and calls logger.info
logging_utils.py lines 422-438: `log_substep` has a 14-line docstring for a 2-line function body (`logger.info(f"\n  {message}")`)
environment.py lines 506-535: `validate_uv_sync_result` has a 19-line docstring with elaborate Example block for a 4-line function that checks two path existences
findings.suggestion: Shrink docstrings on trivial utility functions to a single summary line. Reserve full Args/Returns/Example treatment for public API functions where callers genuinely need that guidance.

findings.summary: log_stage_with_eta and log_resource_usage are defined twice with different signatures across sibling logging modules
evidence: logging_progress.py line 430: `def log_stage_with_eta(stage, current, total, elapsed_time, logger=None)` — 5-param variant
logging_utils.py line 457: `def log_stage_with_eta(stage_num, total_stages, stage_name, pipeline_start=None, logger=None)` — different 5-param variant with different names and semantics
logging_progress.py line 461: `def log_resource_usage(cpu_percent=None, memory_mb=None, logger=None)` — takes floats
logging_utils.py line 486: `def log_resource_usage(stage_name='', logger=None)` — takes a stage name string and calls psutil internally
Both variants are imported into logging_utils.__all__ and re-exported, creating silent shadowing at the package boundary
findings.suggestion: Remove one of each duplicate pair. The logging_utils versions (which call psutil and calculate ETA from pipeline_start) are the more complete implementations. Remove the logging_progress duplicates and update the single caller to use the canonical version.

findings.summary: InputSanitizer (llm/core/sanitization.py) and SecurityValidator (core/security.py) implement nearly identical dangerous-pattern lists and validation logic
evidence: sanitization.py lines 27-50: dangerous_patterns list with 18 regex entries covering prompt injection, code execution, file access, network, LaTeX, SQL, XSS
security.py lines 37-58: dangerous_patterns list with 14 regex entries covering the same categories in nearly identical language
Neither class references the other; LLMClient (core/client.py) imports neither — so both are partially dead at the primary call site
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single sanitization surface. The core/security.py SecurityValidator is already in the core layer and has 3 importers; move any LLM-specific patterns there as an optional extension rather than maintaining two parallel lists.

findings.summary: The logging concern is fragmented across five files in infrastructure/core/ with no sub-package boundary
evidence: logging_utils.py re-imports and re-exports symbols from all four sibling logging files, making it a hub that was manually split without creating a sub-package
The split creates circular-import risk: logging_helpers.py imports from exceptions.py at function-level (line 43: `from infrastructure.core.exceptions import TemplateError`) to avoid the cycle

All 118 importers use `from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import ...` — the split files are implementation-private but live flat in core/ alongside unrelated modules (pipeline, checkpoint, security, health_check)
findings.suggestion: Either move the five logging_* files into an `infrastructure/core/logging/` sub-package with logging_utils.py as its `__init__.py`, or collapse logging_constants, logging_helpers, and logging_formatters back into logging_utils.py since they are never imported directly by external callers.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/ holds 22 files mixing PDF/markdown validation with a 7-file doc_* cluster that has its own internal model layer
evidence: Seven files share the doc_* prefix and form a self-contained cluster: doc_models defines data types, doc_discovery finds files, doc_accuracy/completeness/quality run checks, doc_scanner orchestrates, audit_orchestrator coordinates all of them
check_links.py and repo_scanner.py import from this cluster but live as flat siblings, blurring ownership
infrastructure/validation/__init__.py imports from both the PDF/markdown validation files and the doc cluster, creating a 24-importer hub that mixes two distinct concerns
findings.suggestion: Extract the doc_* files plus audit_orchestrator into `infrastructure/validation/docs/` sub-package with its own `__init__.py`. The PDF/markdown validation files remain in infrastructure/validation/. This aligns placement with change boundaries.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ is an overloaded catch-all housing 26 files across 8+ unrelated capabilities, blurring what 'core' owns
evidence: infrastructure/core/__init__.py exports 251 lines of symbols spanning: exceptions, logging (5 sub-files), progress bars, retry, checkpoint, performance monitoring, health checks, security validation, script discovery, file inventory, pipeline execution, multi-project orchestration — 12 distinct capabilities

SecurityValidator and HealthCheckAPI live in core/ despite having no dependency relationship with the foundation utilities the name 'core' implies; they import from logging_utils but so does every other module
MultiProjectOrchestrator in core/multi_project.py imports PipelineExecutor from core/pipeline.py — both belong in core/ but the orchestration domain is better served by its own boundary
findings.suggestion: Extract security.py + health_check.py into infrastructure/monitoring/ or infrastructure/security/. Extract multi_project.py + pipeline.py into infrastructure/pipeline/. Reserve infrastructure/core/ for true foundation primitives: logging, exceptions, config, file operations, and retry. This reduces the core __init__.py from 251 lines of re-exports to a focused surface.

findings.summary: Widespread sys.path.insert calls across scripts and infrastructure files undermine the declared two-layer import architecture
evidence: 32+ files contain sys.path.insert() calls, including files inside the infrastructure/ layer itself (infrastructure/scientific/templates.py lines 142 and 245, infrastructure/validation/validate_markdown_cli.py line 22, infrastructure/rendering/render_all_cli.py line 11)

CLAUDE.md documents a two-layer architecture where Layer 1 (infrastructure/) is reusable and Layer 2 (projects/) imports from it — but infrastructure files manipulating sys.path means the layer boundary is enforced only by convention, not by package structure

The pyproject.toml and uv.lock approach suggests the project is installed as a package, making the sys.path manipulation redundant in installed contexts but silently required in direct-execution contexts
findings.suggestion: Establish infrastructure as a proper installed package (it has pyproject.toml) and remove sys.path.insert calls from infrastructure/ files entirely. Scripts in scripts/ may retain path manipulation as a thin CLI shim, but infrastructure modules should rely on the installed package path.

findings.summary: infrastructure/steganography/ is a specialized optional feature elevated to a top-level peer alongside core build infrastructure
evidence: infrastructure/__init__.py wraps the steganography import in a try/except with a warnings.warn — acknowledging it is optional — yet it is listed as a top-level sibling of core/, validation/, rendering/, publishing/

The CLAUDE.md architecture description lists it as a module alongside the primary build infrastructure, but its actual role is an optional PDF post-processing step called only after the pipeline completes
7 files in infrastructure/steganography/ (config, overlays, barcodes, metadata, hashing, encryption, core) implement a standalone pipeline with no consumers in the main render or validate paths
findings.suggestion: Move infrastructure/steganography/ under infrastructure/rendering/steganography/ or infrastructure/publishing/steganography/ since it operates on PDFs after rendering. This makes the optional nature apparent from placement and removes a misleading top-level peer that implies equal standing with core infrastructure.
